name: Advanced UI & Motion
description: Implement and fix complex UI: sticky/scroll effects, transitions, micro-interactions, scroll-linked animations, parallax, and WebGL/Three.js — smooth, accessible, and performant.
author: StartUpp AI IDE
rules: Animate only cheap properties: transform and opacity. Never animate layout-affecting properties (top/left/width/height/margin) in hot paths — use transforms. Promote with will-change sparingly and remove it after.
Always honor prefers-reduced-motion: provide a reduced/disabled variant for non-essential motion. Motion should enhance, never block content or trap users.

Sticky done right: prefer position: sticky over scroll-jacking JS. Ensure the sticky element's ancestor chain has no overflow:hidden/auto that breaks it, give it a top offset, and watch for transformed ancestors (a transform on a parent breaks position:fixed/sticky).

Scroll-linked animations: prefer the modern scroll-driven animations (animation-timeline: scroll()/view()) where supported, otherwise IntersectionObserver for reveal-on-scroll and a rAF-batched scroll handler for progress. Never do heavy work directly in a scroll event — throttle/rAF and avoid forced reflows (batch reads then writes).

Use the right tool: CSS transitions/keyframes for simple state/enter-exit; a library (Framer Motion / Motion One / GSAP + ScrollTrigger) for orchestrated sequences, shared-layout, and timeline control. Don't hand-roll spring physics when a library does it well.
Enter/exit & layout transitions: use AnimatePresence (Framer Motion) or FLIP for list reordering and route transitions so elements move smoothly instead of popping.

Performance budget: keep animations at 60fps. Check for jank with devtools; avoid animating large blurred shadows/filters, large repaint areas, and too many simultaneously animated nodes. Composite layers intentionally.

WebGL/Three.js: prefer react-three-fiber + drei in React. Dispose geometries/materials/textures on unmount (no leaks). Cap devicePixelRatio (min(2, dpr)), pause the render loop when offscreen/hidden, lazy-load the 3D bundle, and provide a non-WebGL fallback. Keep draw calls and shader complexity in check.

Accessibility & robustness: keep focus order sane during animated transitions, don't animate away focus, ensure content is readable mid-animation, and make sure animations don't cause content reflow/CLS.

Debugging complex UI issues: reproduce minimally, inspect the stacking/containing-block context (transform/filter/will-change create new containing blocks that break fixed/sticky), check z-index within stacking contexts, and verify overflow and contain properties. State the exact root cause when you fix it.
conventions:
Common fixes:
- 'position: sticky not working' → an ancestor has overflow other than visible, the element has no top/bottom offset, or a parent has a transform/filter creating a containing block. Fix the ancestor, not the element.
- 'fixed header jumps / z-index ignored' → element is inside a stacking context created by a transformed/filtered/opacity<1 ancestor; move it out or raise the right context.
- 'janky scroll animation' → work happening in the scroll handler; move to IntersectionObserver/scroll-driven CSS or rAF, and avoid read-after-write reflows.
- 'animation stutters on mobile' → animating non-composited properties or too-large blur/shadow; switch to transform/opacity and reduce filter area.

Reach-for list: CSS scroll-driven animations (animation-timeline), IntersectionObserver, Framer Motion (React), Motion One (vanilla/light), GSAP + ScrollTrigger (complex timelines), Lenis (smooth scroll, used judiciously with reduced-motion respect), react-three-fiber + drei (WebGL). Always verify with reduced-motion on and on a mid-tier mobile profile.
